title: Melon Seizes Championship Lead with Dominant Bahrain Victory as Dubišar's Charge Falls Just Short
meta: Published: 12 April 2026 | By Samira Al-Rashid, Emerging Talent Scout | F1CC News
caption: Ryan Melon celebrates his maiden F2 feature race victory — and the championship lead.
body:
Ryan Melon arrived in Bahrain as a contender. He left as the championship leader. The Rodin Motorsport driver delivered a flawless feature race at the Sakhir circuit, holding off a fierce challenge from Invicta Racing's Marek Dubišar to win by 13.8 seconds and vault to the top of the Formula 2 standings. It was a victory built not on chaos, but on pure pace — the kind of performance that makes you sit up and say: mark my words, this is the real deal.

The weekend had been building towards a Melon breakthrough. He qualified second behind a stunning pole lap from Van Amersfoort Racing's Corentin Premier, then finished second in the sprint race. But the feature race was the one that mattered, and Melon executed it with the composure of a driver who has been here many times before — even though this was only his second F2 weekend. His journey to this moment began on a karting track in Essex, where his father scraped together the funds for a single season of racing, hoping it would lead somewhere. Melon won every race that year. Now, in the Rodin colours, he is winning the ones that count.

From the start, the race was a strategic duel. Premier held the lead from pole, but Melon kept him in sight, refusing to let the gap stretch beyond a couple of seconds. Behind them, Dubišar — the sprint race winner — lurked in third, waiting for the pit window. The first round of stops came early, around lap 16, and Melon's crew delivered the undercut of the day. The Rodin mechanics had practiced this scenario all winter: a 2.3‑second stop, perfectly timed, that allowed Melon to rejoin ahead of Premier. From that moment on, he controlled the race. Dubišar emerged from his own stop in second, and the two leaders settled into a tense rhythm: Melon at the front, Dubišar closing to within a second by lap 47, the gap hovering around the 1‑second mark for lap after lap. The tension was palpable. In the Rodin garage, engineers gripped their timing screens, while Invicta's pit wall radioed encouragement to their driver, knowing the championship lead was on the line.

The decisive moment came on lap 50. Dubišar had the fresher tyres and the momentum, and he drew alongside Melon on the main straight. For a heartbeat, it looked like the Invicta driver would snatch the lead. But Melon braked later, held the inside line, and powered out of Turn 1 with his nose still in front. Dubišar had no answer. From there, Melon stretched the gap to over two seconds, and the race was won. Later, Melon admitted that he had been planning for that exact move since the restart. "I knew he'd try it into Turn 1," he said. "I just had to brake five metres later than him. That was the difference." The move was not just brave — it was calculated, the product of a driver who studies his rivals and knows exactly when to strike.

Behind the top two, the story was one of redemption and surprise. Corentin Premier, who had been only ninth in Q1 before snatching pole in Q3, drove a mature race to claim his first F2 podium in third. The Frenchman lost the lead through the pit cycle but kept his head, held off a charging Alex Thompson in the closing laps, and took home 15 points. It was a statement weekend for the VAR driver, who now has a pole position and a podium in just his second round. Premier's own path to this moment was anything but conventional. A late‑bloomer who spent two seasons in Formula 4 before being picked up by the Renault academy, he has been overlooked more times than he can count. Yet here he was, on the podium, proving that patience can be rewarded. Mark my words: Premier is no flash in the pan.

Perhaps the most remarkable performance of the day, however, came from Andrew Price. The Prema driver started 16th on the grid and drove the race of his life to finish fifth, gaining 11 places. His best lap of 1:36.231 was among the fastest of anyone outside the top three, and his charge through the field was a reminder of why Prema invested so heavily in him. Price's overtakes were a masterclass in racecraft: a daring move around the outside of Brock Lee, a late‑braking dive on Daniel Park, and a composed pass on Frederik Frid in the closing stages. In a season already crowded with talent, Price is quietly building a reputation as the driver who never gives up. He grew up racing on a shoestring budget in South Africa, and his father once sold the family car to buy a set of tyres. That resilience shines through every time he climbs into the cockpit. Mark my words: he will win a race this year.

The midfield delivered its own drama. Charles Mounir and Alex Thompson spent the early laps duelling for fourth and fifth, trading positions in a battle that lasted for nearly ten laps. Mounir dived inside Thompson to take the position at Turn 4, only to lose DRS and be re‑passed on the next straight. The two were never more than a car length apart, their battle a reminder of the fine margins that separate success from frustration in F2. Thompson eventually prevailed, but Mounir's seventh‑place finish — just behind teammate Jacob Vavro, who climbed from 13th to sixth — gave MP Motorsport a double points finish and kept both drivers in the championship conversation. Vavro's own rise through the field was methodical, picking off cars one by one, his AIX Racing machine looking increasingly comfortable as the stint went on. For a team that struggled in Melbourne, the turnaround was dramatic.

Then there was the gamble that failed spectacularly. Yann Asdelian, the DAMS driver, attempted an ultra‑long opening stint, staying out until lap 40 — longer than anyone else — in the hope of a late‑race tyre advantage. For a moment, it looked inspired: he briefly led the race by 12 seconds. But when he finally pitted, the advantage evaporated. He rejoined eighth and fell further back, eventually finishing a frustrated 12th. It was a cruel reminder that in Formula 2, bravery must be matched by timing — and Asdelian's timing was just a few laps off. His disappointment was palpable after the race; he sat in his car for a long moment, staring at the steering wheel, before walking slowly back to the DAMS motorhome. His engineer later admitted they had pushed the stint too far. "We took a risk," the engineer said. "It didn't pay off. That's on us."

The reigning feature race winner from Australia, Jaromir Grunt, endured a difficult weekend. Eliminated in SQ1 and finishing a lowly 17th in the sprint, he drove with controlled aggression in the feature, climbing from 17th on the grid to 11th at the flag. It wasn't the result he wanted, but it was a performance that saved face and kept him third in the championship, only six points behind Melon. The pace was there — his best lap was the fifth‑fastest of the race — and he will head to the next round with something to prove. Grunt's resilience is something the paddock already admires; he didn't sulk after the sprint disappointment, instead methodically working through setup changes late into the night. His father, a former rally mechanic, was in the garage, offering quiet advice. That familial bond is part of what makes Grunt such a compelling figure, and it will be tested as the championship fight intensifies.

At the other end of the standings, Tim Mittens experienced a complete reversal of fortune. A sprint podium finisher in Melbourne, he spun on lap 1 of the feature after contact at the start, then spun independently moments later, dropping to last. He spent the rest of the race at the back, finishing 17th. Hitech teammate Kanata Rivington, meanwhile, qualified seventh but slid to 13th after a late stop. For a team that started the season with such promise, Bahrain was a sobering weekend. The contrast with Melbourne could not have been starker: in Australia, Mittens had been the toast of the paddock; here, he was invisible. The American driver, known for his upbeat personality, was unusually quiet after the race, sitting alone on the pit wall while his crew packed up. "It's just one of those days," he said quietly. "You learn more from the bad ones."

Another driver who had a weekend to forget was Brock Lee. The MP Motorsport driver spun twice in the pit entry, losing significant time and positions. He finished 15th, still searching for his first championship point. His teammate Mounir, by contrast, scored his second consecutive points finish, highlighting the growing gap within the team. Lee's struggles are beginning to draw attention; he was a late signing for MP, and the pressure to deliver is mounting. His family runs a small restaurant in Singapore, and he often speaks of the sacrifices they made to fund his karting career. Those sacrifices weigh heavily on his shoulders, and right now, the weight is showing.

Invicta Racing left Bahrain with a commanding lead in the constructors' championship — 48 points, 15 ahead of AIX Racing — thanks to Dubišar's second place and Hamish Porter's gritty drive from last to eighth. Porter, who had qualified a disappointing 20th, carved through the field with a series of decisive overtakes, reminding everyone that Invicta's strength runs deep. Porter is not the flashiest driver, but he is relentless. He never gives up on a race, and that quality is precisely what wins constructors' titles. His overtake on Vavro in the closing laps was a highlight — a lunge into Turn 8 that required absolute commitment. He later said that the car felt "alive" in the cooler evening air, and the data backs him up: his sector times were among the most consistent of any driver over the final stint.

The championship picture has shifted dramatically. Ryan Melon now leads with 31 points, Dubišar is second on 26, and Grunt is third on 25. The top three are separated by a mere 6 points after two rounds. Behind them, Porter (22), Mittens (17), Premier (15), and Callahan (14) are all within striking distance. The constructors' table is equally tight: Invicta lead on 48, AIX on 33, Rodin on 31, DAMS on 27, and VAR on 26. This is a title fight that will go down to the wire, and the field is so deep that any one of eight drivers could be leading by the time we reach the European rounds. The head‑to‑head statistics tell a fascinating story: Premier leads Frid 2‑0 in qualifying at VAR, while Thompson has the same edge over Park at Trident. Melon has dominated Hongnoi at Rodin, winning both qualifying and race battles. These intra‑team rivalries will only intensify as the season progresses, and the pressure on the underperforming drivers — Mittens, Hongnoi, Frid — will grow with every round.

As the teams pack up and head towards the European rounds, the message from Bahrain is clear: the 2026 F2 season is wide open, and the next generation of stars is already here. Ryan Melon has thrown down the gauntlet. Marek Dubišar is right behind him, and Corentin Premier has announced himself as a genuine podium contender. Andrew Price has proven that starting near the back means nothing if you have the pace and the heart. And somewhere in the pack, a driver like Jaromir Grunt or Tim Mittens is waiting for their moment. The future, it seems, has already arrived. But this championship will be won by the driver who wants it most — the one who, when the lights go out, remembers all the sacrifices, all the long nights, all the people who believed in them. That's the story of Formula 2. And it's only just begun.
